subject: Chemistry
q: A system is considered to be in thermodynamic equilibrium with its surroundings when
question: A system is considered to be in thermodynamic equilibrium with its surroundings when
options: it has only thermal equilibrium with the surroundings.
it has both thermal equilibrium and chemical equilibrium with the surroundings.
it has thermal, chemical and mechanical equilibrium with the surroundings.
it has thermal and mechanical equilibrium, but does not have chemical equilibrium with the surroundings.
solution:
The correct answer is Option C.

A system is said to be in thermodynamic equilibrium with its surroundings only when three conditions are satisfied together.

First, there should be thermal equilibrium, which means the temperature of the system is the same as that of the surroundings. If the temperatures differ, heat will flow between them, and the system cannot remain in a steady state.

Second, there should be mechanical equilibrium, meaning the pressure of the system matches the pressure of the surroundings. If the pressures are unequal, the system will either expand or get compressed, so its state will keep changing.

Third, there should be chemical equilibrium, which means the chemical composition of the system does not change with time. There should be no chemical reaction going on and no diffusion of matter between the system and surroundings.

Only when all three — thermal, mechanical, and chemical equilibrium — exist at the same time, we say the system is in complete thermodynamic equilibrium.

So the correct choice is option C.

subject: Chemistry
q:
When two moles of Hydrogen atoms join together to form a mole of hydrogen molecules in a closed rigid vessel with diathermic walls.

H (g) + H (g) → H2 (g)

Which of the following is correct?
question:
When two moles of Hydrogen atoms join together to form a mole of hydrogen molecules in a closed rigid vessel with diathermic walls.

H (g) + H (g) → H2 (g)

Which of the following is correct?
solution:
The correct option is Option B: $\Delta U$ is negative.

In the reaction,

$$ \text{H(g)}+\text{H(g)} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{(g)} $$

two hydrogen atoms combine to form a hydrogen molecule. During this process, an H–H bond is formed. Bond formation releases energy, so the system loses energy.

The vessel is rigid, so its volume does not change.

$$ \Delta V = 0 $$

Work done is

$$ w = -P_{\text{ext}}\Delta V $$

So,

$$ w = 0 $$

Hence, Option A is not correct because $w$ is not less than zero.

According to the first law of thermodynamics,

$$ \Delta U = q + w $$

Since $w=0$,

$$ \Delta U = q_v $$

The reaction releases heat, so heat of the system is negative:

$$ q_{\text{system}} < 0 $$

So,

$$ \Delta U < 0 $$

Also, the surroundings receive the heat released by the system, so

$$ q_{\text{surrounding}} > 0 $$

Thus, the only correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option B: } \Delta U \text{ is negative}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following statements is true?
question: Which one of the following statements is true?
options: Heat is a thermodynamic property of a system.
Work is a thermodynamic property of a system.
Work done by a conservative force depends on the path followed.
The heat change in a chemical reaction is a path-independent physical quantity.
solution:
Option D is correct.

Heat and work are not thermodynamic properties of a system. They are not state functions. They appear only during a process, when energy is transferred.

So,

  • Heat is not a thermodynamic property of the system.

  • Work is also not a thermodynamic property of the system.

Hence options A and B are incorrect.

For a conservative force, the work done depends only on the initial and final positions, not on the path followed.

For example, work done by gravitational force is path independent.

So, the statement

“Work done by a conservative force is path function”

is incorrect. Option C is wrong.

In a chemical reaction, the heat change at constant pressure is equal to enthalpy change:

$$ q_p = \Delta H $$

Enthalpy is a state function, so $\Delta H$ depends only on the initial and final states of the reaction. It does not depend on the path followed. This is also the basis of Hess’s law.

So the correct statement is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: A system absorbs 100 kJ heat in the process shown in the figure. What is $\Delta U$ for the system?
question: A system absorbs 100 kJ heat in the process shown in the figure. What is $\Delta U$ for the system?
solution:
The work done in this process is equal to the negative of the area under the $P-V$ graph, because the volume is increasing.

The pressure changes from $1 \text{ bar}$ to $2 \text{ bar}$, so the average pressure is

$\dfrac{1+2}{2}=1.5 \text{ bar}$

The change in volume is $1 \text{ m}^3$. So,

$W=-1 \times \dfrac{1+2}{2}=-1.5 \text{ bar}\cdot \text{m}^3$

Now convert $\text{bar}\cdot \text{m}^3$ into kJ:

$1 \text{ bar}\cdot \text{m}^3=100 \text{ kJ}$

So,

$W=-1.5 \times 100=-150 \text{ kJ}$

The system absorbs heat, so

$q=+100 \text{ kJ}$

Using the first law of thermodynamics,

$\Delta U=q+W$

$\Delta U=100+(-150)=-50 \text{ kJ}$

So, $\Delta U=-50 \text{ kJ}$.

subject: Chemistry
q: For an isothermal process, the essential condition is
question: For an isothermal process, the essential condition is
solution:
For an isothermal process, the temperature remains constant throughout the process.

So at every small stage of the process, the change in temperature is zero:

$$ dT = 0 $$

This is stronger than only saying

$$ \Delta T = 0 $$

because $\Delta T = 0$ only means the initial and final temperatures are the same. The temperature may still change in between. But in an isothermal process, temperature must remain constant at every moment.

So the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D: } dT = 0} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: In an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas. Select wrong statement :
question: In an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas. Select wrong statement :
options: there is no change in the temperature of the gas
there is no change in the internal energy of the gas
the work done by the gas is equal to the heat supplied to the gas
the work done by the gas is equal to the change in its internal energy
solution:
In an isothermal expansion, the temperature of the gas remains constant.

For an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature. So if temperature does not change,

$$ \Delta U = 0 $$

Using the first law of thermodynamics,

$$ Q = \Delta U + W $$

Since $\Delta U = 0$,

$$ Q = W $$

So, the heat supplied to the gas is equal to the work done by the gas.

Now checking the options:

A. There is no change in temperature — correct.

B. There is no change in internal energy — correct.

C. Work done by the gas is equal to heat supplied — correct.

D. Work done by the gas is equal to change in internal energy — wrong, because $\Delta U = 0$ but work done during expansion is not zero.

So the wrong statement is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: The volume of a system becomes twice its original volume on the absorption of 300 cal of heat. The work done on the surrounding was found to be 200 cal. What is $\Delta U$ for the system?
question: The volume of a system becomes twice its original volume on the absorption of 300 cal of heat. The work done on the surrounding was found to be 200 cal. What is $\Delta U$ for the system?
solution:
The system absorbs heat, so heat given to the system is positive:

$$ q = +300 \text{ cal} $$

The work is done on the surroundings. This means the system is doing work. So, according to the NCERT sign convention, work done by the system is taken as negative:

$$ w = -200 \text{ cal} $$

Using the first law of thermodynamics,

$$ \Delta U = q + w $$

Substituting the values,

$$ \Delta U = 300 + (-200) $$

$$ \Delta U = 100 \text{ cal} $$

So, the change in internal energy of the system is:

$$ \boxed{100 \text{ cal}} $$

Correct option: C.

subject: Chemistry
q: For one mole of a Van der Waals gas undergoing reversible isothermal expansion from volume $V_1$ to $V_2$ at temperature $T$ K, the magnitude of the work done is
question: For one mole of a Van der Waals gas undergoing reversible isothermal expansion from volume $V_1$ to $V_2$ at temperature $T$ K, the magnitude of the work done is
solution:
For a reversible expansion, the external pressure is equal to the pressure of the gas at every stage. So we can write the work as

$W=-\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV$

For one mole of a Van der Waals gas, the pressure is

$P=\frac{RT}{V-b}-\frac{a}{V^2}$

Substitute this value of $P$ in the work expression:

$\begin{aligned} W &=-\int_{V_1}^{V_2}\left(\frac{RT}{V-b}-\frac{a}{V^2}\right)dV \\\\ &=-\left[RT\ln\frac{V_2-b}{V_1-b}+a\left(\frac{1}{V_2}-\frac{1}{V_1}\right)\right]\end{aligned}$

Since the question asks for the magnitude of work done, we take the positive value:

$|W|=RT\ln\frac{V_2-b}{V_1-b}+a\left(\frac{1}{V_2}-\frac{1}{V_1}\right)$

subject: Chemistry
q: A gas is compressed from 325 ml to 125 ml under a constant pressure of 2 bar. During this process, 124 J of heat is taken out from the gas. What will be the change in its internal energy?
question: A gas is compressed from 325 ml to 125 ml under a constant pressure of 2 bar. During this process, 124 J of heat is taken out from the gas. What will be the change in its internal energy?
solution:
The gas is being compressed, so work is done on the gas. That means work will be positive for the system.

Work done on the gas:

$$w = -p\Delta V$$

Here, $\Delta V = V_2 - V_1 = 125 - 325 = -200 \text{ ml} = -0.2 \text{ L} = -0.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$

Pressure $p = 2 \text{ bar} = 2 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$

So,

$$w = -(2 \times 10^{5})(-0.2 \times 10^{-3}) = +40 \text{ J}$$

Heat is removed from the gas, so $q = -124 \text{ J}$.

Using the first law of thermodynamics:

$$\Delta U = q + w = -124 + 40 = -84 \text{ J}$$

The change in internal energy is $-84 \text{ J}$, which matches Option B.

subject: Chemistry
q:
One mole of an ideal gas undergoes the following cyclic process.

(i) Isochoric heating from $(P_1, V_1, T_1)$ to double temperature.

(ii) Isobaric expansion to double volume.

(iii) Linear expansion (on $P$–$V$ curve) to $(P_1, 8V_1)$.

(iv) Isobaric compression to initial state.

If $T_1 = 300\;\text{K}$, then the magnitude of net work done by the gas in the cyclic process is
question:
One mole of an ideal gas undergoes the following cyclic process.

(i) Isochoric heating from $(P_1, V_1, T_1)$ to double temperature.

(ii) Isobaric expansion to double volume.

(iii) Linear expansion (on $P$–$V$ curve) to $(P_1, 8V_1)$.

(iv) Isobaric compression to initial state.

If $T_1 = 300\;\text{K}$, then the magnitude of net work done by the gas in the cyclic process is
solution:
For one mole of an ideal gas, the initial state is

$$ (P_1, V_1, T_1) $$

Using ideal gas equation,

$$ P_1V_1 = RT_1 $$

Given,

$$ T_1 = 300\ \text{K} $$

So,

$$ P_1V_1 = R \times 300 $$

Taking $R = 2\ \text{cal mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$,

$$ P_1V_1 = 600\ \text{cal} $$

In process (i), volume is constant, so work done is zero:

$$ W_1 = 0 $$

In process (ii), pressure is constant at $2P_1$ and volume changes from $V_1$ to $2V_1$.

$$ W_2 = P\Delta V $$

$$ W_2 = 2P_1(2V_1 - V_1) $$

$$ W_2 = 2P_1V_1 $$

In process (iii), the gas expands along a straight line on the $P$-$V$ graph from $(2P_1, 2V_1)$ to $(P_1, 8V_1)$.

For a straight line process,

$$ W_3 = \frac{P_{\text{initial}} + P_{\text{final}}}{2} \Delta V $$

$$ W_3 = \frac{2P_1 + P_1}{2}(8V_1 - 2V_1) $$

$$ W_3 = \frac{3P_1}{2} \times 6V_1 $$

$$ W_3 = 9P_1V_1 $$

In process (iv), the gas is compressed isobarically at pressure $P_1$ from $8V_1$ to $V_1$.

$$ W_4 = P_1(V_1 - 8V_1) $$

$$ W_4 = -7P_1V_1 $$

So, net work done is

$$ W_{\text{net}} = W_1 + W_2 + W_3 + W_4 $$

$$ W_{\text{net}} = 0 + 2P_1V_1 + 9P_1V_1 - 7P_1V_1 $$

$$ W_{\text{net}} = 4P_1V_1 $$

Since $P_1V_1 = 600\ \text{cal}$,

$$ W_{\text{net}} = 4 \times 600 $$

$$ W_{\text{net}} = 2400\ \text{cal} $$

So, the magnitude of net work done by the gas is

$$ \boxed{2400\ \text{cal}} $$

Correct option: A.

subject: Chemistry
q: For a given change of state, a system goes from the initial state to the final state through path I. The heat absorbed and the work done are 10 kcal and 0 erg, respectively. For the same change of state through path II, the corresponding values are 11 kcal and $0.5 w_{\max }$, where $w_{\max }$ denotes the work done when the change is carried out reversibly. The magnitude of $w_{\text {max }}$, in J, is $(1 \mathrm{cal}=4.2 \mathrm{~J})$
question: For a given change of state, a system goes from the initial state to the final state through path I. The heat absorbed and the work done are 10 kcal and 0 erg, respectively. For the same change of state through path II, the corresponding values are 11 kcal and $0.5 w_{\max }$, where $w_{\max }$ denotes the work done when the change is carried out reversibly. The magnitude of $w_{\text {max }}$, in J, is $(1 \mathrm{cal}=4.2 \mathrm{~J})$
solution:
According to the first law of thermodynamics, the change in internal energy is given by:

$$ \Delta U = q + w $$

where $q$ is the heat absorbed by the system and $w$ is the work done on the system.

Let's first look at Path I. We are given:

Heat absorbed, $q_1 = 10 \text{ kcal}$

Work done, $w_1 = 0 \text{ erg} = 0 \text{ kcal}$

Using the first law, we can find the change in internal energy for the process:

$$ \Delta U = q_1 + w_1 = 10 \text{ kcal} + 0 = 10 \text{ kcal} $$

Now consider Path II. Because this path leads to the exact same final state from the same initial state, the overall change in internal energy $\Delta U$ must be exactly the same as it was for Path I. So, $\Delta U = 10 \text{ kcal}$ here as well.

For Path II, we are given:

Heat absorbed, $q_2 = 11 \text{ kcal}$

Work done, $w_2 = 0.5 w_{\max}$

$$ \Delta U = q_2 + w_2 $$

$$ 10 \text{ kcal} = 11 \text{ kcal} + 0.5 w_{\max} $$

$$ 0.5 w_{\max} = 10 \text{ kcal} - 11 \text{ kcal} $$

$$ 0.5 w_{\max} = -1 \text{ kcal} $$

$$ w_{\max} = -2 \text{ kcal} $$

$1 \text{ cal} = 4.2 \text{ J}$, which means $1 \text{ kcal} = 1000 \times 4.2 \text{ J} = 4200 \text{ J}$.

$$ |w_{\max}| = 2 \times 4200 \text{ J} = 8400 \text{ J} $$

The magnitude of the reversible work done is $8400 \text{ J}$.

subject: Chemistry
q:
How many of the following are state functions?

(a) Internal energy

(b) Volume

(c) Heat

(e) Temperature

(f) Work

(d) Enthalpy

(g) Molar heat capacity
question:
How many of the following are state functions?

(a) Internal energy

(b) Volume

(c) Heat

(e) Temperature

(f) Work

(d) Enthalpy

(g) Molar heat capacity
solution:
State functions depend only on the initial and final state of the system, not on the path followed.

(a) Internal energy, $U$

It depends only on the state of the system.

So, it is a state function.

(b) Volume, $V$

Volume describes the state of the system.

So, it is a state function.

(c) Heat, $q$

Heat depends on the path of the process.

So, it is not a state function.

(e) Temperature, $T$

Temperature is a property of the state of the system.

So, it is a state function.

(f) Work, $w$

Work depends on the path followed.

So, it is not a state function.

(d) Enthalpy, $H$

Enthalpy is defined as

$$ H = U + PV $$

Since $U$, $P$, and $V$ are state functions, $H$ is also a state function.

(g) Molar heat capacity

Molar heat capacity involves heat supplied, and heat depends on the path. Unless a specific condition like constant pressure or constant volume is given, it is not treated as a state function.

So the state functions are:

$$ U,\ V,\ T,\ H $$

Hence, the number of state functions is

$$ \boxed{4} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: The maximum work done when pressure of $n$ moles of $H_2$ was reduced from 20 atm to 1 atm at constant temperature of 273 K is found to be 8180 calories. What is the value of $n ?$
question: The maximum work done when pressure of $n$ moles of $H_2$ was reduced from 20 atm to 1 atm at constant temperature of 273 K is found to be 8180 calories. What is the value of $n ?$
solution:
For maximum work, the expansion must be reversible and isothermal.

For an ideal gas undergoing reversible isothermal expansion,

$$ W_{\text{max}} = nRT \ln \frac{P_1}{P_2} $$

Here,

$$ P_1 = 20 \text{ atm}, \quad P_2 = 1 \text{ atm} $$

$$ T = 273 \text{ K} $$

$$ W_{\text{max}} = 8180 \text{ cal} $$

Using $R = 2 \text{ cal mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$,

$$ 8180 = n \times 2 \times 273 \times \ln \frac{20}{1} $$

Now,

$$ \ln 20 = 2.303 \log 20 $$

and

$$ \log 20 = 1.301 $$

So,

$$ \ln 20 = 2.303 \times 1.301 \approx 3 $$

Thus,

$$ 8180 = n \times 2 \times 273 \times 3 $$

$$ 8180 = n \times 1638 $$

$$ n = \frac{8180}{1638} \approx 5 $$

So, the value of $n$ is

$$ \boxed{5} $$

subject: Chemistry
q:
How many of the following are path functions?

(a) Internal energy

(b) Volume

(c) Heat

(e) Temperature

(f) Work

(g) Molar heat capacity

(d) Enthalpy
question:
How many of the following are path functions?

(a) Internal energy

(b) Volume

(c) Heat

(e) Temperature

(f) Work

(g) Molar heat capacity

(d) Enthalpy
solution:
The path functions among the given quantities are:

$$\text{Heat, Work, and Molar heat capacity}$$

So, the number of path functions is

$$\boxed{3}$$

Internal energy, volume, temperature, and enthalpy are state functions. Their values depend only on the state of the system, not on how the system reached that state.

Heat and work are path functions because their values depend on the path followed during a process.

Molar heat capacity is also path dependent because heat absorbed depends on the process. For example, molar heat capacity at constant volume is $C_V$, while at constant pressure it is $C_P$, and these are different.

So the path functions are:

$$ (c)\ \text{Heat}, \quad (f)\ \text{Work}, \quad (g)\ \text{Molar heat capacity} $$

Hence, the answer is

$$\boxed{3}$$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Find the number of incorrect statements.

(i) Thermodynamics deals only with the total energy of a system.

(ii) The $1^{\text {st }}$ law of thermodynamics may be used for a single particle present inside a vessel.

(iii) Several thermodynamic properties cannot be determined in absolute terms; therefore, their changes are used in calculations.

(iv) Thermodynamics does not explain whether a chemical reaction is feasible or not.

(v) If the surroundings remain in equilibrium with the system at every stage, the process is known as reversible.

(vi) Thermodynamics is able to tell the time needed to reach equilibrium.
question:
Find the number of incorrect statements.

(i) Thermodynamics deals only with the total energy of a system.

(ii) The $1^{\text {st }}$ law of thermodynamics may be used for a single particle present inside a vessel.

(iii) Several thermodynamic properties cannot be determined in absolute terms; therefore, their changes are used in calculations.

(iv) Thermodynamics does not explain whether a chemical reaction is feasible or not.

(v) If the surroundings remain in equilibrium with the system at every stage, the process is known as reversible.

(vi) Thermodynamics is able to tell the time needed to reach equilibrium.
solution:
(i) Thermodynamics is not concerned only with total energy. It also deals with changes in energy, heat, work, entropy, enthalpy, free energy and many other properties. So this statement is false.

(ii) The first law of thermodynamics applies to a system as a whole, which contains a large number of particles. It cannot be applied to a single individual particle, because thermodynamic quantities like heat, internal energy etc. are bulk (macroscopic) properties. So this statement is false.

(iii) Properties like internal energy ($U$) and enthalpy ($H$) cannot be measured in absolute terms. That is why we always calculate the change, such as $\Delta U$ and $\Delta H$. So this statement is true.

(iv) Feasibility of a reaction is actually decided by thermodynamics using $\Delta G$. If $\Delta G < 0$, the reaction is feasible. So saying thermodynamics cannot explain feasibility is false.

(v) A reversible process is one in which the system and surroundings remain in equilibrium at every stage of the process. So this statement is true.

(vi) Thermodynamics tells us whether a process is feasible or not, but it does not tell us how fast it will occur or the time needed to reach equilibrium. That comes under chemical kinetics. So this statement is false.

The false statements are (i), (ii), (iv) and (vi).

Number of false statements $= 4$.
